turns.content: I'm building an automation tool and I keep running into issues with naming conflicts. I have a macro_rules! that generates unique identifiers by concatenating strings, like my_prefix_01 or my_prefix_02, but when I try to use paste::paste!("my_prefix", "01") in my code it fails because the compiler can't resolve the identifier before the macro expands.

That's a classic gotcha with macros. When you write a macro that generates identifiers, those identifiers don't exist at compile time until the macro is fully expanded. The paste crate solves this by taking string literals and returning them as Rust identifiers directly in the generated code. You can use it like paste::paste!("my_prefix", "01") to get my_prefix_01 immediately available for use.
Wait, so I should just replace all those string concatenations in my macro_rules! with paste::paste!(...) calls? That seems like a straightforward substitution right?

You're mostly on the right track but there's an important distinction. You cannot simply substitute paste::paste!(...) into your existing macro_rules! definition because macros are defined at compile time and they don't execute code until expansion happens. The paste crate is designed to be used inside a macro, not as a replacement for string interpolation logic within one.
Ah okay so I can't just swap in the function call? What exactly should I do instead if I want to avoid name collisions?

You have two main options here. The first is to use a unique prefix based on the module path or crate name so that generated names are globally unique. For example, you could generate something like crate::module_name_01 instead of just my_prefix_01. Alternatively, you can actually use paste! within your macro_rules! definition itself - not as a replacement but as part of the expansion logic.
Can you clarify that second point? If I use paste inside my macro_rules!, doesn't that still require the macro to expand before it runs?

Exactly, and that's where the magic happens. When your macro_rules! contains a call like paste::paste!("my_prefix", "01"), the macro itself doesn't execute code yet - but when you actually USE that macro in your code, it expands into actual Rust code with those identifiers already resolved. So paste! acts as a bridge between string literals and real identifiers at expansion time.
So I should define my macro_rules! to call paste::paste!(...) for the variable names, then use that macro in my automation tool's functions?

Precisely. You define a macro like my_macro!("prefix", "01") which internally calls paste::paste!("prefix", "01") to create the identifier, then you use that macro in your automation tool's code where you need those unique variable names. This prevents naming conflicts because each call generates a distinct identifier.
That makes sense now. So paste! isn't just for concatenating strings but actually for generating usable identifiers in generated code?

Exactly, that's the core purpose. It takes string literals and produces valid Rust identifiers that can be used immediately after macro expansion without needing to wait for further resolution or deal with name collisions.
What's the difference between a regular macro_rules! and using the paste crate for concatenating identifiers?

Think of it like this: a standard macro_rules! is more like a text editor that finds patterns in your code and swaps them out, whereas the paste crate is a compiler helper that actually generates new tokens at compile time. For example, if you need to create unique function names by joining module prefixes with item identifiers, paste! lets you write something like paste!(concat!(MODULE_PREFIX, "_", ID)) which produces the actual identifier string in the final binary instead of just text substitution.

I'm struggling to see why I can't just use normal string interpolation in my function definitions. It seems like paste! is only useful for generating unique names, but what if I need something more complex?

You're right that paste! shines when you need to generate unique identifiers dynamically, but it's also great for building function signatures or type parameters where you want to avoid naming conflicts. Have you considered whether your issue is actually about collision between generated names rather than just simple string joining?
